name: Human Rights
title: 'I am trying to live': Haitians in Mexico seek community despite broken immigration systems
description:
TheUN ranks Mexico among the top five countries for receiving the highest numbers of new asylum seekers.
Meanwhile, Mexican immigration authorities do not allow asylum seekers to leave the city where they begin their asylum process, and they must report for check-ins every two weeks.
Most asylum seekers there said they were unable to find work, and when they did, it was typically in low-paid jobs off the books.
But almost a dozen Haitian asylum seekers in Tapachula said their cases had extended past 90 days.
As gang violence in Haiti continued to rise and more Haitians fled to Mexico, their asylum applications are rarely successful.
text:
A year ago, when Jean Baptiste Gensley stepped off a bus in Tapachula, Mexico’s southern city on the border with Guatemala, he carried a small backpack and the hope that his journey was finally over.

In his native Haiti, Gensley, 37, worked as a radio journalist and social worker, analyzing the effects of gang violence in some of Port-au-Prince’s most dangerous neighborhoods. With time, as his research led to police intervention, he caught the attention of the city’s gangs.

In November 2024, during a period of escalating violence in the Haitian capital, gang members entered the compound where Gensley lived.

“They burned the radio station, my home and many other things in the area,” he said in an interview in Tapachula. They even killed his dog.

It was time to get out. After escaping Haiti and leaving his wife and 11-year-old son in the neighboring Dominican Republic for safety, Gensley continued by himself, fleeing on rural back roads deep into the countryside. He paid a local agency to take him to Punta Cana, a resort town on the eastern shores of the island. From Punta Cana, he flew to Quito, Ecuador’s capital – then took a plane to El Salvador.

On buses, in cars and on the back of motorcycle taxis, Gensley made his way hundreds of miles north. Finally, he crossed into Mexico.

He said: “I had no money left. All my funds were spent during the journey.”

Gensley had the US in his sights but decided it was more feasible to choose Mexico as his final destination.

Like many fleeing Haiti in recent years, he had initially thought the US should be his goal, with better prospects. But a hardening of attitudes and restrictions under Joe Biden and an extreme crackdown on immigration and asylum by Donald Trump ultimately convinced him that Mexico would be a much safer bet.

Four days after his arrival in Tapachula, Gensley checked in at the Mexican commission for refugee assistance (Comar), where the agency told him he could get a work permit and right of residence. He was encouraged, but, in fact, months slipped by without any further news from Comar, leaving the Haitian journalist in a restless limbo. Days blended into one another and a settled future felt painfully out of reach.

As thousands of other Haitians continued to arrive in Tapachula, the main entry point for migrants traveling north through Central America, many have found themselves trapped in a tangle of hostile or dysfunctional immigration systems involving Mexico, the US and the UN. There are few smooth paths forward, whether they are aiming to settle there or head for central or northern Mexico, or the US.

Trump has repeatedly expressed hostility towards Haiti, while also defunding international programs, including some that affect Mexico, and shutting out asylum seekers, refugees and migrants at the US-Mexico border.

Since 2010, Haitians in the US had been eligible for temporary protected status (TPS) after the devastating 2010 earthquake and, later, escalating violence and instability, including the 2021 assassination of the Haitian president Jovenel Moïse that prompted multiple extensions of the program. But the Trump administration is trying to terminate TPS for Haitians.

In early February, a lawsuit temporarily blocked the administration’s attempt to end Haitians’ eligibility for TPS, which would have left up to 350,000 Haitians stranded without legal status. Even so, ongoing uncertainty, including a drastic cut to the numbers of people accepted as refugees each year, has made it increasingly difficult for Haitians to reach or stay in the US.

Mexico is increasingly becoming a destination for many fleeing war, oppression, crushing poverty, gang violence or combinations of those problems. However, as a relative newcomer like Gensley quickly found out, Mexico’s immigration system is shaped by US pressure.

Since returning to office, Trump has used the threat of tariffs to challenge Mexico’s immigration and border policies – including the announcement of a 25% tariff in February 2025 that was reversed weeks later. Last month, Trump again announced a 10% global tariff on most imported goods. Mexican products categorized as compliant with the three-country trade agreement called USMCA are now excluded from the tariff.

At the same time, cuts to US funding for international aid organizations have further strained Mexico’s asylum infrastructure. In 2025, as part of the administration’s dismantling of USAID, funding to the UN was slashed, forcing the UN high commissioner for refugees (UNHCR) to suspend its financial support for Mexico’s Comar.

Until then, the US had provided more than 86% of the funding for UNHCR’s work in Mexico, according to a 2024 UNHCR funding report. In the aftermath of the severe cuts, Comar, which itself relies on the UN for roughly 60% of its budget, has struggled to process asylum applications, bringing the system to a near-standstill.

In Tapachula, a patchwork of migration-focused organizations now does what it can to fill the gaps. For years, UNHCR played a central role, supporting housing for hundreds of migrants in shelters across Mexico. After the funding cuts, several shelters were forced to close.

According to UNHCR, about 11 million people worldwide are losing such support because of the cuts, leaving many asylum seekers in dreary conditions, poverty and prolonged instability. TheUN ranks Mexico among the top five countries for receiving the highest numbers of new asylum seekers.

“Before President Trump, UNHCR gave help, not for everyone, but they helped,” Gensley said. “Now, with President Trump, UNHCR helps almost nobody.”

Melanie Gallant, UNHCR spokesperson for Latin America and the Caribbean, wrote in a statement: “Despite a funding cut of more than 50%, UNHCR continues to prioritize essential support: helping Comar keep asylum procedures running, providing interpretation when possible, offering legal information and orientation, and assisting people with heightened protection needs.”

UNCHR’s cuts to services in Tapachula, Gallant added, reflect global budget pressures – “not a shift in UNHCR’s commitment to supporting Mexico”.

Meanwhile, Mexican immigration authorities do not allow asylum seekers to leave the city where they begin their asylum process, and they must report for check-ins every two weeks. Most asylum seekers there said they were unable to find work, and when they did, it was typically in low-paid jobs off the books.

Jesús Zamarrón, an immigration lawyer in Tapachula who works closely with asylum seekers, said Mexican authorities were enforcing US immigration priorities under pressure from the threat of tariffs. By keeping migrants far from US territory, cities like Tapachula function as an extension, or externalization, of the US border, he said.

Zamarrón added that Mexico had increasingly relied on its own southern border with Guatemala as a sort of “containment zone” to stem northward migration.

Zamarrón said: “As a law firm, we have experienced delays in the immigration regularization processes for Haitian and Cuban populations. Especially with the Haitian population, due to a lack of interpreters.”

Some Haitian immigrants arrive with a basic level of Spanish after living in the Dominican Republic or Central and South America before traveling to Mexico – but most do not. Haitians generally speak Creole, French or both languages.

As a result, they require interpreters to complete their asylum interviews with officials from Comar.

But throughout 2025, Comar had to let go of many of its workers, including interpreters for asylum interviews, because of the funding cuts. This has lengthened the asylum process significantly – particularly for Haitians, who make up the largest group of non-Spanish speakers entering Mexico.

According to Mexican law, the asylum process should take about 45 days and can be extended to 90. But almost a dozen Haitian asylum seekers in Tapachula said their cases had extended past 90 days. Some, like Gensley, have had their process extended for more than nine months, leaving them unable to obtain the work permit that is a standard element of the asylum process.

Finally, Gensley received a date for his asylum interview and completed it last October.

“I saw it as a gift from God,” he said.

In late November, after nine months of waiting, Gensley received the news that his asylum application had been approved. Now, he is trying to earn enough money to move to Tijuana, where job prospects are better and there is a more established Haitian community.

He hopes to reunite with his family as soon as possible – but does not know when that will be.

As gang violence in Haiti continued to rise and more Haitians fled to Mexico, their asylum applications are rarely successful. In a 2024 report, a coalition of human rights organizations noted that between 2021 and 2023, only 4.6% of Haitians’ asylum applications were approved by Comar.

Christelle, an elementary school teacher from Haiti who requested that her last name not be shared in order not to risk her asylum case, says she has spent 10 months in Tapachula without being called in for an interview with Comar.

“When we come here, everything happens step by step, and we have to wait,” she said. “I am trying to live, but life is very hard here.”

Since 2024, Comar has applied the Cartagena declaration, a UN agreement that expanded the definition of “refugee” to applicants from Haiti. When used correctly, the declaration should also entail the faster processing of asylum claims.

But if Haitians continue to wait months in limbo, the question arises if Cartagena is being fairly applied to them.

“If the ability to even access the procedures and the process isn’t there, then that is a big challenge,” said Gabrielle Apollon, director of the Haitian Immigrant Rights Project at the New York University School of Law’s Global Justice Clinic and coordinator of the Hemispheric Network for Haitian Migrants’ Rights.

Comar did not respond to a request for comment on access to the system.

On a typical weekday, hundreds of Haitians gather around Tapachula’s Bicentennial Park and the street markets that spread around the city center. Some sell bottles of water or Haitian fried fish with pressed plantains that they have cooked in crowded apartments. Others look after the stalls of Mexican merchants.

Newcomers have found comfort in the Haitian community as their cases undergo the same delays.

Every few days, David Corrielan, a TikToker from Haiti who spent years living in the Dominican Republic, posts a new video on his channel, El Haitiano Mexicano. In interviews and video guides, he shares a view of life in Tapachula for Haitians, and Mexicans curious about their new neighbors.

“This account is to help my Haitian siblings live well in Mexico, and here in Tapachula,” Corrielan said. “I want to share Haitian culture and the Haitian language with Mexicans.”

Two and a half thousand miles north is an example of how things could look for the Haitian diaspora stuck in Tapachula. Tijuana, on Mexico’s northern border with San Diego, California, has a substantial Haitian community that stems from earlier waves of migration north.

Since at least 2016, Tijuana has been a stopping point for thousands of Haitians trying to reach the US. In 2021, Haitian migrants were greeted with brute force by American border officials. Fearing deportation back to Haiti, many asylum seekers waited in migrant camps and crowded shelters across sprawling Tijuana.

Over time, many of them settled in the city, building a community there. Some, like Vivianne Petit Frére, learned Spanish and obtained citizenship after they had children born in Mexico. Like many other Haitians, Petit Frére came to Tijuana in 2021 fleeing violence in the aftermath of Moïse’s assassination.

At the time, Petit Frére hoped to reach the US – seeking the “American Dream”, she said. But as American authorities deported Haitian asylum seekers directly to the country they had fled, she decided it was safer to stay in Tijuana.

“When I arrived in Tijuana, I found opportunity – and I woke up from the ‘American dream’. Now, I’m living my [version of the] American dream,” Petit Frére said.

Petit Frére bustled around the dining room of her restaurant, Lakou Lakay, near downtown Tijuana, serving a long table of researchers from the nearby university, Colegio de la Frontera Norte, who have stopped by for Haitian fried fish, meat, and rice and beans.

They sat under a row of brightly colored placards. One described the four principles of Vodou, and another declared the restaurant as a space “where Haitian identity proudly blossoms on Mexican soil”.

Through her business, and work with the Haitian community – she is finishing her social work degree – Petit Frére has become a go-to source of information and resources for Haitians living in Tijuana, looking for information on navigating visa issues, money transfers or just a bite of food.

“It’s that voice that’s saying, ‘I’m here, I’m a part of this, I belong,’” she said. “That’s when I started to put down roots.”

The obstacles are many, but Mexico can still be the answer.

Petit Frère said: “What they’re looking for isn’t [necessarily] the United States. It’s stability, whether economic or moral – whatever makes them feel good.”

Back in Tapachula, Gensley and other Haitians who have fled the violence of their home country wait, day after day, for a piece of that stability.

Gensley holds on to the hope that he will reunite with his family – but for now, until he can establish himself more solidly, it’s not possible.

“I don’t know if things will get better now that I have my documents,” he said. “I have to remain patient. That’s it.”

name: Human Rights
title: Protesters target Citizens Bank over its financing of ICE prisons - Steve Ahlquist's Substack
description:
“We are here, outside Citizens Bank, to demand that they stop financing ICE prisons,” said Raya, a student organizer with Brown Rise Up in Providence.
“Citizens Bank provides the loans that CoreCivic and Geo Group need to build infrastructure to detain tens of thousands more people.
“Today, we’re telling corporations like Citizens Bank that we won’t stand by as they quietly fund ICE detention centers.
“Citizens Bank, directly behind us, lends upwards of $2.5 billion to fund these ICE detention centers.
So I started, right away, to close my bank account at Citizens Bank.
text:
“We are here, outside Citizens Bank, to demand that they stop financing ICE prisons,” said Raya, a student organizer with Brown Rise Up in Providence. “As we witness ICE [Immigration and Customs Enforcement] continue to terrorize our immigrant communities across the country, we have to understand that ICE doesn’t operate in isolation. It relies on airlines to fly deportation flights, rental car companies for vehicles, and private prison companies for detention centers. These private prison companies that Citizens Bank finances are key parts of the deportation machine.

“ICE can’t kidnap our neighbors without a place to detain them, so today we are joining people across the country to target enablers of ICE terror. There are over 60 protests taking place today at Citizens Bank locations across the country. Four are in Rhode Island. This is the second day of action this year, and the largest ever protest against Citizens Bank. We are part of a movement, and it is growing.”

A three-month-old campaign to protest – and stop – Citizens Bank’s financial support to companies operating immigration detention centers for ICE got much bigger on Saturday as concerned community groups held around 60 peaceful protests in a dozen East Coast and Midwest states to protest Citizens Bank’s longstanding financial support to the county’s two largest prison companies, CoreCivic and The GEO Group, which operate dozens of ICE detention facilities across the country. That’s a significant increase from the three-dozen protests on the January 24 region-wide protests against the Providence-based bank. The De-ICE Citizens Bank Coalition organized the national protests.

Here’s the video:

“Headquartered right here in Providence, Citizens Bank continues to be the only major lender to the country’s two largest private prison companies, CoreCivic and The Geo Group,” said Adelaide, an organizer with Jewish Voice for Peace, Rhode Island. “Citizens Bank provides the loans that CoreCivic and Geo Group need to build infrastructure to detain tens of thousands more people. Citizens says it is dedicated to fostering strong communities and social responsibility, but its longstanding support for prison companies and ICE detention centers is exactly the opposite.

“By organizing account holders to move their money and with all of us putting pressure on the bank’s leaders, together we can end their complicity in ICE violence. Our movements have done it before. In 2019, in response to grassroots activists and shareholder pressure, eight of the country’s largest banks, including JPMorgan and Barclays, ended their relationships with the private prison industry. Citizens Bank refused and has only deepened its ties.

“But that also means that Core Civic and Geo Group are more dependent than ever on financial services from Citizens to fuel mass incarceration and to run and build ICE detention facilities.”

Four protests were held in Rhode Island, outside Narragansett, Providence, Wakefield, and Westerly Citizens Bank Locations. These local protests were endorsed by AMOR (Alianza para Movilizar Nuestra Resistencia/Alliance to Mobilize our Resistance); Brown Rise Up; Cranston Forward; Indivisible METRO-RI; Jewish Voice for Peace-Rhode Island; Lil’ Rhody Visibility Brigade; RI-WTF! Resistance Collective; South County Resistance; South County Rising; and West Bay Blue Wave.

“We have leverage, and we must use it now,” said Raya. “Just this Tuesday, ICE detained a man near the Garrahy Judicial Complex downtown. Five ICE vehicles blocked the street while agents took a man from his car, and this isn’t a one-time tragedy. ICE consistently kidnaps people who are showing up to their hearings. They show up at the courthouse every week, sometimes multiple times a week, and are active in neighborhoods across Providence. The Garrahy Judicial Complex is only a 20-minute walk from this location.

“ICE is not an abstract threat,” continued Raya. “They’re here now, menacing our neighbors, but we aren’t just letting that happen. People are patrolling courthouses and neighborhoods in Providence every weekday. Mobilizing as part of the Deportation Defense Network [401-675-1414], which has been mobilizing people across Rhode Island to push back against ICE.”

With $45 billion in new funding from last year’s budget bill, ICE and the Department of Homeland Security are planning to double the country’s detention capacity from a year ago, to over 100,000 beds, relying heavily on the same private prison contractors to expand their footprints. This fast-growing detention system, which had fewer than 7,500 beds in 1995, separates families, endangers detained individuals, and erodes fundamental rights to safety and due process. Banks provide the financial backbone for more arrests and detention of immigrants. Cutting off that support weakens the companies that are handsomely profiting from human rights abuses.

“AMOR started in 2017 to fight back against an administration that promised to ramp up attacks against our immigrant community,” said Sophia, who works at AMOR. “We’re here today at Citizens Bank to show that we will not accept the participation of any entity that supports ICE infrastructure. From us, you will not get a dime.

“Citizens Bank helped CoreCivic and The Geo Group, the largest private prison corporations providing beds for ICE, access hundreds of millions of dollars for their activities. We demand that Citizens Bank cut ties with CoreCivic and Geo Group now. These private prison companies detained tens of thousands of people for ICE and are responsible, like many prisons across the U.S., for forced labor, solitary confinement, and medical neglect, which on Monday led to the 10th death of an individual in ICE custody this year, all while their revenue skyrocketed in 2025.

“They’ve also blocked laws passed by California, Washington, and New Jersey that were meant to end private prison contracts with ICE in their states. CoreCivic and GEO Group sued the states to protect their monetary interests, to protect their billions of dollars in ICE contracts built on the suffering and separation of families. When detention centers are owned by companies whose sole interest is staying open and filling beds, they will inevitably ignore gross living conditions and the human rights abuses we’ve seen coming out of these prisons.

“Abolishing ICE and ending immigrant detention does not stop at private prisons. Rhode Island has its own quasi-public private detention center with its own story of abuse and ongoing urgent needs from ICE detainees, ranging from medical care to special diets to guaranteed purified water. The Wyatt Detention Center in Central Falls, a 20-minute drive from here, is accountable only to its investors, who prioritize profit over the inhumane detention of our neighbors.

“We have a chance to shut the Wyatt down with House Bill 7436 and Senate Bill 2278 that would end contracts between municipalities and ICE for immigrant detention. Please follow AMOR on Instagram for how to support these bills, how to give testimony for them, and to join the Deportation Defense Network, a statewide movement to defend itself against ICE terror. So if you see ICE, call 401-675-1414, and hundreds of community members will mobilize to push ICE out. If you’re not already involved, or if you want to get involved, you can take out your phone right now and text ‘defend’ to 401-675-1414.

“To end all immigrant detention, we must not cooperate. ICE is logistically able to terrorize our community and us because of the infrastructure supplied by participating businesses: airlines aid in deportations, banks fund private prisons, car companies rent vehicles, and ICE agents use hotels. Our legislators can do something about it by fighting for all the anti-ICE-related bills this year, and we can close our bank accounts with Citizens Bank and tell everyone we know to do the same.”

“Today, we’re standing in coalition with over 60 other protests across New England,” said Levi, co-president of Brown Rise Up. “Today, we’re telling corporations like Citizens Bank that we won’t stand by as they quietly fund ICE detention centers. Since Brown Rise Up formed last October, we’ve remained dedicated to fighting against the Trump Administration’s authoritarian attacks. They’ve come for our school, our most vulnerable students, and our right to protest like we are today. But we know that fighting authoritarianism can’t stop at just our school and our students. One of the most powerful arms of Trump’s terror is the brutality of ICE that we see in the news and on our streets. We know that to stop the fascist agenda that exploits the working people of our country, we need to stand together, and that starts by telling these corporations and the billionaires that line their pockets with blood money, know.: That we won’t stand for them bankrolling ICE detention centers and funding the cruelty of our administration.

“Citizens Bank, directly behind us, lends upwards of $2.5 billion to fund these ICE detention centers. These are spaces where people are routinely denied medical care, access to lawyers, and their basic human dignity. The executives at Citizens know this. They’re directly complicit in the violence we’ve seen against our communities from the murders in Minneapolis this past January to the ICE kidnappings just blocks from here at the Providence Courthouse. From its very inception in 2003, ICE has been a force of terror against our immigrant communities, and now, the Trump Administration has made it abundantly clear that it’ll stop at nothing to terrorize our immigrant neighbors. We need to get loud and get organized.

“Last month, 1500 people in our community stood together in solidarity against ICE’s brutality and murder in Minneapolis. People mobilized nationwide. And yes, people were scared and tired of watching the violence of ICE play out on the news. But, more importantly, people realize that we have the power to shut this down. We have the power to grind the machine of fascism to a halt. I am in this fight because it takes all of us, and because I see my international friends who are too scared to protest or say anything that could be seen as criticizing the government. I’m fed up with how the Trump Administration scapegoats immigrants for our country’s problems, while gutting SNAP and Medicaid and starting wars that kill children in other countries.

“After years and years of feeling helpless and a feeling like all I could do was read the news of these horrible things happening to people around me, I now know that something has to give. Yes, it can feel like the problem is so deeply entrenched in the system that it’s impossible to do anything, but that is precisely the moment when you have to stand up and get organized, because it’s only through the power of the people that we can win.

“Citizens Bank has made its choice. They’ve chosen the side of billionaires and fascists. They’ve chosen to bankroll ICE’s cruelty. But we also have a choice: Today, we can stand together in community here and in coalition across the nation and tell them no. We can organize against the authoritarians who are coming after our communities and the corporations like Citizens who enable them to, and we can shut it down.”

“I’m a Rhode Islander, born and raised. Like a lot of people who grew up here, my very first bank account, when I was 16, was at Citizens Bank,” said Michaela, an organizer with Indivisible Metro Rhode Island. “Opening that bank account felt like a Rhode Island rite of passage. Citizens Bank was a staple in our communities, a place families trusted, a place that claimed to invest in the neighborhoods that we call home. But let me be clear about something: A Rhode Island staple does not fund ICE terror.

“Citizens Bank relies on Rhode Islanders. They rely on our paychecks, savings, mortgages, and trust. Today, we’re here to tell them something very simple: They cannot rely on us as long as they choose to fund the detention and terrorizing of our neighbors. While eight other major banks walked away from private prison companies after public pressure, Citizens Bank didn’t step back. They doubled down. In fact, they strengthened their relationship with companies that profit from detention, deportation, and family separation.

“That’s why we’re here today, not just in Rhode Island, but across 60 branches, 12 states, and DC. Thousands of us are standing together with one message: Citizens Bank, stop funding ICE terror. Stop harming the very communities that you claim to serve because immigrants are our neighbors, friends, coworkers, parents, and students, and no bank should profit from locking them in cages.

“Here’s what we do next: To everyone standing here today, do something. Write Citizens Bank a letter, send an email, call corporate headquarters, post on social media, or share a post on social media. Start a petition, sign a petition. And if they still refuse to listen, close your accounts. Move your money. Organize people and businesses in your community to do the same because our money should never be used to put human beings in cages.

“And to Citizens Bank, we say this: If you truly want to be a Rhode Island institution, and if you truly want the trust of the communities you claim to serve, show us. Stand with all Rhode Islanders. End your relationship with private prison groups. Stop financing ICE detention facilities and make a public commitment today that you will never fund ICE terror again.”

“The struggle against ICE in Providence and across the U.S. is tied in so many ways to the struggle against apartheid and genocide in Palestine and against this week’s illegal assault on Iran,” said Zach, representing Jewish Voice for Peace, Rhode Island. “The tactics and technologies of repression, abuse, and surveillance that we are seeing deployed by ICE today, developed against Black and brown communities in the U.S., have been honed in Gaza and the West Bank against Palestinians for decades. I mean that literally.

“Since 2003, over a thousand ICE and CBP (Customs and Border Protection) agents have been sent to train with Israeli police and military, sharing notes on how to repress and abuse our neighbors. The Department of Homeland Security has an entire office tasked with managing joint training and programs with the Israeli government and tech institutions, and it’s not just tactics of militarism being shared; it’s also the technologies and investments that enable these abuses. They’re tested in Gaza, the West Bank, and U.S. prisons and detention centers, and deployed here in our communities.

“Citizens Bank, based here in our own city, is invested in this infrastructure, enabling and profiting from the incarceration and deportation of our neighbors, facilitated by private prison companies like GEO Group and CoreCivic. People in these prisons don’t have access to adequate healthcare. They’re held without legal process, denied food, and put into solitary confinement. These are the conditions that Palestinians have been familiar with for decades. In the last two and a half years, the restriction of food and aid into Gaza has caused devastating starvation. And right now, thousands of Palestinians are held in administrative detention, meaning they have had no trial, no access to legal counsel, and oftentimes don’t even know the crimes they’re charged with. These are the same conditions our neighbors are facing in ICE jails today.

“Citizens Bank’s complicity goes beyond the prison walls. Citizens Bank’s seventh-largest investment, worth millions of dollars, is in Palantir, a surveillance tech company currently using AI to build kidnapping lists for ICE and kill lists for the U.S. and Israeli militaries in Palestine and Iran. ICE uses the same technology and tools as a military apparatus currently carrying out a genocide. The only kind of society that can stomach bombing tents, ripping people out of their cars, and caging thousands of people is one where some people are seen as less than human.

“The U.S. and Israel are using the same playbook of dehumanization. The violent companies locking up our neighbors in Minneapolis or nearby in Central Falls, and bombing our loved ones in Gaza, the West Bank, and Tehran, means every dollar that Citizens Bank makes from these investments is stained with blood. We will not tolerate this, and together we will show them that profiting from the destruction of our communities here in Rhode Island and abroad will cost them more than they’re willing to pay.”

“The same logic of dehumanization underlies the U.S. government’s terrorizing of Black and brown communities here at home and abroad. U.S. intervention has never brought and never will bring peace, democracy, or liberation. Look at Iraq, Libya, and Afghanistan,” said Adelaide. “Look at our government’s stalwart support of genocide in Gaza for 883 days. We will never defeat fascism or militarization here at home without also pushing back against every single one of the U.S.’s illegal and imperial wars, including the bombing of Ecuador this week and the escalating U.S.-Israeli war on Iran. In just one week, since last Saturday, the U.S. has already spent over $5 billion bombing girls’ schools and hospitals. That money, our money, could be providing healthcare, housing, education, and jobs here, not murdering families across the region and displacing 300,000 people in South Lebanon and carpet bombing Tehran.

“Despite the blatant lies of those in power, the American people are overwhelmingly against the war on Iran. We can’t take that for granted. It’s a huge change in public opinion over the last two decades, from 2003 to today. But the billionaires trying to colonize and control the planet will not stop their murderous rampage unless we are loud, strategic, and organized as part of building the largest anti-war movement this country has ever seen.

“It’s important that we all show up boldly to end this war, including by protest. This weekend, Rhode Island Senator Jack Reed needs to hear from every single one of us. He holds significant power to block this war as the ranking member of the Senate Armed Services Committee. Right now, he is refusing to commit to blocking Trump’s request for $50 billion more to kill Iranians. We, the organized grassroots of Rhode Island, pushed him, just a few weeks ago, to vote to block funding to ICE, and we can do it again.”

“I haven’t lived here all that long, but I got a bank account at Citizens Bank,” said Janine, an organizer with Cranston Forward. “They seem like a nice company, right? They had all these pictures of families playing on the beach with their dogs and making plans with their children. When I learned about their involvement with CoreCivic and Geo Group, I was like, ‘My bank supports ICE prisons and prisons for profit?’ I thought about those pictures of families having a really good time. And I thought about the other pictures I’ve been seeing of families being torn apart and people taken off to prison.

“That’s when I decided that I had to do something. So I started, right away, to close my bank account at Citizens Bank. And it’s not that big a deal. It’s been a pain, but it’s not like being shot down in the streets, right? This is something everybody can do. It’s one little thing I can do that will make a difference. I wrote a letter to them, butthey didn’t care too much about my 2,000 bucks in the bank, and they didn’t write me back. That’s when I decided to get a new bank account. When money talks, banks listen, because that’s what they do. We need to take our money and walk out of Citizens Bank.”

“Before we close out, it’s imperative to remember that ICE terror isn’t new,” said Raya. “ICE has been attacking immigrant communities since its birth as an agency in the racist war on terror of the early 2000s. This type of state-sanctioned violence isn’t new to Black and brown communities in the U.S., who have long been excluded from American democracy. Just look at the convict lease system. In the aftermath of slavery, as many as 200,000 black people in the U.S. were imprisoned, and their labor was leased out to private companies - corporate slavery - sometimes for the ‘crime’ of not having a job.

“The U.S. criminal justice system continues to overwhelmingly incarcerate people of color, more often than not, people who are considered guilty before being proven innocent. We also have to reflect on why so many immigrants are forced to come to the U.S. in the first place. Oftentimes, they’re forced to flee conditions created by U.S. economic and military intervention, and then they’re met with criminalization and threats of deportation. We have to remember that Obama deported three million people during his presidency, that Democrats voted to keep funding genocide in Palestine, and that our systems are built to serve the interests of the richest and exploit minorities to retain power and control. They are built to divide us, but we are refusing to be divided.

“As we move forward and continue to build this movement against ICE violence, we have to remember that we are standing on the shoulders of years and years of organizing by grassroots immigrant activists against ICE, including before Trump. We have to use our awakened and active consciousness that no human is illegal to make sure that the world we are building is one in which there is no space for caging human beings.

“I ask you all to reflect and to fight for a radically better world that serves people’s needs, not the deportation and war machines. We are seeing an explosion in mass detentions across the country, and ICE is actively planning to expand its detention capacity to over a hundred thousand beds. Just this morning, there were reports that three American citizens and three green card holders, including at least one birthright citizen, were detained by CBP when returning to Chicago Airport. Their whereabouts are currently unknown. They’re in one of these detention facilities.

“I don’t know about you, but I am angry. I am done seeing photos of abandoned cars whose drivers have been ripped out of them and high school students being attacked by masked agents. I am tired of hearing about people dying in detention without adequate healthcare. I am ready to try and stop that, to hit somewhere where ICE is vulnerable.

“It’s time to end Citizens Bank’s critical support to ensure that CoreCivic and Geo Group don’t have the money they need to build more infrastructure for detention and death. We’re going to keep bringing people into this movement. We are going to keep building, and I believe that we will win.”

name: Policy
title: Poll: Trump struggles on immigration, prices and Iran as Democrats hold a midterm edge
description:
Against that backdrop, Democrats have a 6-point lead in the fight for control of Congress, where Republicans currently hold narrow majorities in both the House and Senate.
The survey was conducted Feb. 27 to March 3, as the war with Iran began and the first primaries of the midterm elections occurred.
Trump also receives mixed reviews on two of his signature issues: border security and immigration.
Shortly before Trump’s election in 2024, 50% said immigration helps more than hurts, while 43% said it hurts more than helps.
When it comes to other issues, Republicans continue to hold a significant advantage on dealing with border security, crime and immigration over Democrats, but their edge has gone down since NBC News’ poll in late October.
text:
Majorities of registered voters disapprove of how President Donald Trump has handled the issues that defined the first months of a tumultuous midterm election year, as Democrats maintain an advantage in the battle for control of Congress, according to a new national NBC News poll.

Voters give Trump poor marks for his handling of immigration after his administration surged federal agents into the heart of American cities and immigration officers in Minnesota killed two U.S. citizens in January. They are down on his tariffs after the Supreme Court struck down his main tariff program in February and Trump later reimposed some of those levies.

And they don’t like his actions on Iran, with the U.S. now at war with the nation after Trump ordered strikes starting last weekend — strikes a majority say should not have happened.

Meanwhile, voters continue to disapprove sharply of Trump’s handling of inflation and the cost of living, issues that were key to his 2024 victory and remain among Americans’ top concerns heading into the midterm elections.

Against that backdrop, Democrats have a 6-point lead in the fight for control of Congress, where Republicans currently hold narrow majorities in both the House and Senate. The survey was conducted Feb. 27 to March 3, as the war with Iran began and the first primaries of the midterm elections occurred.

There are bright spots for the president and warning signs for Democrats, too. Trump continues to receive stronger marks for his handling of border security, a longtime signature issue, according to the poll, and voters still trust his party more than Democrats to handle both border security and immigration. There are signs of support for his focus on illegal voting, too, as the president seeks to make that a central political issue, though documented instances of voting by ineligible people are rare.

And Republicans narrow the Democratic advantage when voters are asked to weigh which party they think would do a better job of handling their most important issues. Most voters, including a sizable portion of self-identified Democrats, continue to view the Democratic Party negatively, continuing a trend in the aftermath of the 2024 election. The Republican Party also gets negative ratings, though not to the same degree.

“The electoral data points, together with views of President Trump and majorities disapproving of his actions on Iran and inflation, and the profound levels of anger at the establishment — all point to an American electorate that is once again fed up with those in power and looking for change wherever they can find it,” said Democratic pollster Jeff Horwitt of Hart Research Associates, who conducted the survey alongside Republican pollster Bill McInturff of Public Opinion Strategies.

Trump’s struggles persist

Trump’s job approval rating is at 44% — essentially stable since the NBC News poll conducted in October, when it was 43% among registered voters — with 54% of voters disapproving of Trump’s performance as president. A majority (56%) also say Trump is either bringing the wrong kind of change or not bringing change at all, while 41% say he is bringing the right kind of change.

Across five issues tested, voters give the president their lowest marks on the economy, with 62% disapproving of Trump’s handling of inflation and the cost of living and 36% approving. It’s an issue Democrats are trying to capitalize on heading into the midterms, after the party’s candidates found success on the issue in 2025, even as Trump has tried to change the narrative on the economy.

Nearly half of voters (48%) say Trump’s policies have hurt the economy, while 35% say they have helped and 16% say they have not made much of a difference. It’s a turnaround from Trump’s first term, when more voters said his policies helped (41%) than hurt (26%).

Voters are particularly frustrated by Trump’s tariffs, with 55% saying they have hurt the economy and 33% saying they have helped.

Just over 1 in 4 voters (27%) say their own financial situation is getting better, while 38% say it is getting worse and 35% say it is staying about the same.

Trump also receives mixed reviews on two of his signature issues: border security and immigration.

While 53% of voters approve of Trump’s handling of border security, 54% disapprove of Trump’s handling of immigration amid pushback on the administration’s mass deportation policies and the deaths of Renee Good and Alex Pretti in Minnesota earlier this year. The survey was conducted before Trump fired Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem, the administration’s most public face of the policies carried out by Immigration and Customs Enforcement as well as Border Patrol.

More than half of voters say they have negative views of ICE, while 38% have positive views of the agency.

But that doesn’t mean voters want to bar local law enforcement from working with federal immigration officials, the policy of so-called sanctuary cities. A plurality of voters (43%) say they have negative feelings about sanctuary cities, while 33% view them positively and 10% were unsure how they felt about them.

American voters’ overall feelings about immigration continue to fluctuate in the Trump era, with 60% saying immigration helps the United States more than it hurts, while 32% say immigration does more damage to the country. Shortly before Trump’s election in 2024, 50% said immigration helps more than hurts, while 43% said it hurts more than helps.

The survey also shows division on another issue that is top of mind for Trump: elections. Trump has been pushing new legislation to overhaul the country’s voting laws, repeating his false claims that the 2020 election was stolen.

A slim majority (51%) say they are more concerned with making sure that those who are not eligible to vote cannot vote, while 47% say they are more concerned with ensuring that everyone who wants to vote can do so.

That’s a significant shift from an NBC News poll in April 2021, when 58% said they were more concerned with ensuring everyone who wants to vote can do so, while 38% said they wanted to ensure that those who are not eligible to vote cannot vote.

Democrats lead in the fight for Congress

As both parties begin to nominate candidates for this year’s midterm elections, Democrats continue to hold an edge on the generic congressional ballot: 50% of registered voters say they’d prefer Democrats win control of Congress this fall, compared to 44% who prefer Republican control. That’s just a slight change from October, the last time this question was asked on the NBC News survey.

The 6-point edge sits in the middle of the range seen at this point in past election cycles — higher than the 2-point Republican advantage in a March 2022 NBC News poll and lower than the 10-point Democrats advantage in a March 2018 NBC News poll. Democrats need a net gain of only three seats to capture the majority in the U.S. House, while the Senate remains more of an uphill battle.

Voters are already plugged into the elections, with 64% rating their interest in the election as either a 9 or a 10 on a 10-point scale — higher than usual for this point in an election cycle, and more in line with typical levels of enthusiasm in NBC News polling conducted just before an election day.

Seventy-four percent of Democrats say they have that high interest (a 9 or 10 out of 10) in the fall’s election, compared to 61% of Republicans.

Two issues are weighing on Americans’ minds more than others as they prepare to make their voices heard at the ballot box: inflation and the cost of living, and threats to democracy. Each of these issues was named by 26% of voters as the most important issue facing the country. Another 13% chose immigration and border security, while 11% chose jobs and the economy.

When voters’ top two issues were combined, 48% chose inflation and costs, 36% chose threats to democracy, 25% chose immigration and border security and 24% chose jobs and the economy, while 21% chose the cost of health care premiums, 16% chose availability of affordable housing and 10% chose crime.

For the second poll in a row, Democrats have pulled even with Republicans on the question of which party would do better dealing with the economy, with 40% of registered voters choosing each party. It’s Republicans’ worst showing on that question in NBC News polling since December 2017.

“Democrats had an advantage on the economy” in previous years when they’ve enjoyed big election wins, said McInturff, the Republican pollster from Public Opinion Strategies. “So what we’re used to is, when Republicans are in deep trouble, it’s usually because Republicans start losing the economic agenda.”

McInturff cautioned that while “this is not a great survey for Republicans” as it relates to the midterms, the GOP is not in as bad shape as it was ahead of the 2006 and 2018 elections, two midterms where Democrats dominated.

He pointed to an about-even split in how many voters identify with each party. When asked which party voters think would do better handling their top issues, Democrats had a 48%-45% advantage — an edge, but a smaller one than on the question of whom voters would like to see control Congress.

When it comes to other issues, Republicans continue to hold a significant advantage on dealing with border security, crime and immigration over Democrats, but their edge has gone down since NBC News’ poll in late October.

Then, Republicans had a 31-point advantage on which party voters think would do a better job dealing with border security and an 18-point advantage on dealing with immigration, but those advantages have dipped to 27 points and 12 points in the latest survey.

Democrats retained their edge on protecting constitutional rights (+7) and protecting democracy (+11), while their lead on dealing with health care dropped slightly, from 23 points in October to 20 points in the new survey.

text:
President Trump on Sunday threatened to not sign any bills into law until the Safeguard American Voter Eligibility (SAVE) America Act is approved by the Senate, doubling down on his push to change voting requirements ahead of the midterm elections.

“I, as President, will not sign other Bills until this is passed, AND NOT THE WATERED DOWN VERSION – GO FOR THE GOLD: MUST SHOW VOTER I.D. & PROOF OF CITIZENSHIP: NO MAIL-IN BALLOTS EXCEPT FOR MILITARY – ILLNESS, DISABILITY…,” the president wrote in his Sunday morning Truth Social post.

The White House did not respond to a request for comment on whether the president will sign a bill to fund the Department of Homeland Security amid the partial government shutdown.

The leader has pressed Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) to use a talking filibuster to pass the bill, which was approved by the House in February.

The filibuster would force Democrats to speak continuously on the Senate floor to delay a vote on the bill. But, if they cede the floor, Republicans could circumvent the need for a 60-vote majority and pass the measure with just 51 votes.

On Sunday, Trump again urged Thune to use this tactic to get the bill across the finish line.

“Great Job by hard working Scott Pressler on Fox & Friends talking about using the Filibuster, or Talking Filibuster, in order to pass THE SAVE AMERICA ACT, an 88% issue with ALL VOTERS,” the president wrote, referring to the conservative activist.

“It must be done immediately. It supersedes everything else. MUST GO TO THE FRONT OF THE LINE,” he continued.

Thune, who ran for Senate majority leader under the commitment to uphold the 60-vote threshold, has not budged on his stance despite Trump’s pressure.

The SAVE America Act has ample support from House Republicans and has already passed the lower chamber three separate times over the years. Democrats, however, have pushed back on the measure, claiming it would pose challenges for registered voters and potentially inhibit people from heading to the polls.

The Hill has reached out to the White House as well as Thune’s and House Speaker Mike Johnson’s (R-La.) offices for comment.

Updated at 12:55 p.m. EDT

text:
Republican Sen. Thom Tillis (N.C.) said Sunday his party has “lost the debate” on immigration, days after President Trump tapped Sen. Markwayne Mullin (R-Okla.) to replace Department of Homeland Security (DHS) Secretary Kristi Noem.

“We’ve lost the debate over immigration and deportations. I believe that we should deport everyone that we can find that came across the borders during the Biden administration. But we’ve got to be smart, use our limited resources, go after the most dangerous first,” Tillis told host Jake Tapper on CNN’s “State of the Union.”

“It didn’t look like that happened under Noem,” he noted.

Tillis, who is not seeking reelection, repeatedly criticized Noem after federal immigration officers fatally shot U.S. citizens Renee Good and Alex Pretti in Minneapolis earlier this year.

Protests against the Trump administration’s sweeping immigration crackdown erupted nationwide in the wake of those incidents. An AP-NORC survey last month also found that 52 percent of respondents believe President Trump has gone too far in his deportation efforts.

“We’re beginning to get the American people to think that deporting people is wrong. It’s the exact opposite,” Tillis told Noem during a Senate Judiciary Committee hearing earlier this week.

“The way you’re going about deporting them is wrong, the fact that you can’t admit to a mistake, which looks like under investigation, it’s going to prove that Ms. Good and Mr. Pretti probably should not have been shot in the face and in the back,” he said.

As for Mullin, who Tillis praised Thursday after Trump nominated the Oklahoma Republican to helm DHS, the retiring senator hopes his Senate colleague can “fix what’s broken there and build on what’s right” within the department.

“I believe that Markwayne recognizes that it’s quality over quantity,” Tillis added. “We want to go after the most dangerous people — the gang members, the drug traffickers, the murders, the rapists, the [people] that Noem promised that we would go after and she simply didn’t deliver.”

name: Policy
title: Poll: Trump struggles on immigration, prices and Iran as Democrats hold a midterm edge
description:
Against that backdrop, Democrats hold a 6-point lead in the fight for control of Congress, where Republicans currently hold narrow majorities in both the House and Senate.
Trump continues to receive stronger marks for his handling of border security, a longtime signature issue, according to the poll.
Trump also receives mixed reviews on two of his signature issues: border security and immigration.
Shortly before Trump’s election in 2024, 50% said immigration helps more than hurts, while 43% said it hurts more than helps.
When it comes to other issues, Republicans continue to hold a significant advantage on dealing with border security, crime and immigration over Democrats, but their edge has gone down since NBC News’ poll in late October.
text:
Majorities of registered voters disapprove of President Donald Trump’s handling of the issues that defined the first months of a tumultuous midterm election year, as Democrats maintain an advantage in the battle for control of Congress, according to a new national NBC News poll.

Voters give Trump poor marks for how he has handled immigration, after his administration surged federal agents into the heart of American cities and immigration officers in Minnesota killed two U.S. citizens in January. They are down on his tariffs, after the Supreme Court struck down his main tariff program in February and Trump later re-imposed some of those levies.

And they don’t like his handling of Iran, with the U.S. now at war with the nation after Trump ordered strikes starting last weekend — strikes a majority say should not have happened.

Meanwhile, voters continue to disapprove sharply of Trump’s handling of inflation and the cost of living, issues that were key to his 2024 victory and remain among Americans’ top concerns heading into the midterm elections.

Against that backdrop, Democrats hold a 6-point lead in the fight for control of Congress, where Republicans currently hold narrow majorities in both the House and Senate. The survey was conducted February 27 to March 3, as the war with Iran began and the first primaries of the midterm elections occurred.

There are bright spots for the president and warning signs for Democrats, too. Trump continues to receive stronger marks for his handling of border security, a longtime signature issue, according to the poll. There are signs of support for his focus on illegal voting, too, as the president seeks to make that a central political issue, though documented instances of voting by ineligible people are rare.

And Republicans narrow the Democratic advantage when voters are asked to weigh which party they think would do a better job of handling their most important issues. Most voters, including a sizable portion of self-identified Democrats, continue to view the Democratic Party negatively, continuing a trend in the aftermath of the 2024 election. The Republican Party also gets negative ratings, though not to the same degree.

“The electoral data points, together with views of President Trump and majorities disapproving of his actions on Iran and inflation, and the profound levels of anger at the establishment — all point to an American electorate that is once again fed up with those in power and looking for change wherever they can find it,” said Democratic pollster Jeff Horwitt, who conducted the survey alongside Republican pollster Bill McInturff of Public Opinion Strategies.

Trump’s struggles persist

Trump’s job approval rating is at 44% — essentially stable since the NBC News poll conducted in October, when it was 43% among registered voters — with 54% of voters disapproving of Trump’s performance as president. A majority (56%) also say Trump is either bringing the wrong kind of change or not bringing change at all, while 41% say he is bringing the right kind of change.

Across five issues tested, voters give the president their lowest marks on the economy, with 62% disapproving of Trump’s handling of inflation and the cost of living and 36% approving. It’s an issue Democrats are trying to capitalize on heading into the midterms, after the party’s candidates found success on the issue in 2025, even as Trump has tried to change the narrative on the economy.

Nearly half of voters (48%) say Trump’s policies have hurt the economy, while 35% say they have helped and 16% say they have not made much of a difference. It’s a turnaround from Trump’s first term, when more voters said his policies helped.

Voters are particularly frustrated by Trump’s tariffs, with 55% saying they have hurt the economy and 33% saying they have helped.

Just over one in four voters (27%) say their own financial situation is getting better, while 38% say it is getting worse and 35% say it is staying about the same.

Trump also receives mixed reviews on two of his signature issues: border security and immigration.

While 53% of voters approve of Trump’s handling of border security, 54% disapprove of Trump’s handling of immigration, amid pushback to the administration’s mass deportation policies and the deaths of Renee Good and Alex Pretti in Minnesota earlier this year. The survey was conducted before Trump fired Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem, the administration’s most public face of the policies carried out by Immigration and Customs Enforcement as well as Border Patrol

More than half of voters say they have negative views of ICE, while 38% have positive views of the agency.

But that doesn’t mean voters want to bar local law enforcement from working with federal immigration officials, the policy of so-called sanctuary cities. A plurality of voters (43%) say they have negative feelings about sanctuary cities, while 33% view them positively and 10% were unsure how they felt about them.

American voters’ overall feelings about immigration continue to fluctuate in the Trump era, with 60% saying immigration helps the United States more than it hurts, while 32% say immigration does more damage to the country. Shortly before Trump’s election in 2024, 50% said immigration helps more than hurts, while 43% said it hurts more than helps.

The survey also shows division on another issue that is top of mind for Trump: elections. Trump has been pushing new legislation to overhaul the country’s voting laws, repeating his false claims that the 2020 election was stolen.

A slim majority (51%) say they are more concerned with making sure that those who are not eligible to vote cannot vote, while 47% say they are more concerned with ensuring that everyone who wants to vote can do so.

That’s a significant shift from an NBC News poll in April 2021, when 58% said they were more concerned with ensuring everyone who wants to vote can do so, while 38% said they wanted to ensure that those who are not eligible to vote cannot vote.

Democrats lead in the fight for Congress

As both parties begin to nominate candidates for this year’s midterm elections, Democrats continue to hold an edge on the generic congressional ballot: 50% of registered voters say they’d prefer Democrats win control of Congress this fall, compared to 44% who prefer Republican control. That’s just a slight change from October, the last time this question was asked on the NBC News survey.

The 6-point edge sits in the middle of the range seen at this point in past election cycles — higher than the 2-point Republican advantage in a March 2022 NBC News poll and lower than the 10-point Democrats advantage in a March 2018 NBC News poll. Democrats need a net gain of only 3 seats to capture the majority in the U.S. House, while the Senate remains more of an uphill battle.

Voters are already plugged into the elections, with 64% rating their interest in the election as either a nine or a 10 on a 10-point scale — higher than usual for this point in an election cycle, and more in line with typical levels of enthusiasm in NBC News polling conducted just before an election day.

Seventy-four percent of Democrats say they have that high interest (a nine or 10 out of 10) in the fall’s election, compared to 61% of Republicans.

Two issues are weighing on Americans’ minds more than others as they prepare to make their voices heard at the ballot box: inflation and the cost of living, as well as threats to democracy. Each of these issues was named by 26% of voters as the most important issue facing the country. Another 13% chose immigration and border security, while 11% chose jobs and the economy.

When voters’ top two issues were combined, 48% chose inflation and costs, 36% chose threats to democracy, 24% chose jobs and the economy, while 21% chose the cost of health care premiums,16% chose availability of affordable housing and 10% chose crime.

For the second poll in a row, Democrats have pulled even with Republicans on the question of which party would do better dealing with the economy, with 40% of registered voters choosing each party. It’s Republicans’ worst showing on that question in NBC News polling since December 2017.

“Democrats had an advantage on the economy” in previous years when they’ve enjoyed big election wins, said McInturff, the Republican pollster from Public Opinion Strategies. “So what we’re used to is, when Republicans are in deep trouble, it’s usually because Republicans start losing the economic agenda.”

McInturff cautioned that while “this is not a great survey for Republicans” as it relates to the midterms, the GOP is not in as bad shape as it was ahead of the 2006 and 2018 elections, two midterms where Democrats dominated.

He pointed to an about-even split in how many voters identify with each party. When asked which party voters think would do better handling their top issues, Democrats had a 48%-45% advantage — an edge, but a smaller one than on the question of who voters would like to see control Congress.

When it comes to other issues, Republicans continue to hold a significant advantage on dealing with border security, crime and immigration over Democrats, but their edge has gone down since NBC News’ poll in late October.

Then, Republicans had a 31-point advantage on which party voters think would do a better job dealing with border security and an 18-point advantage on dealing with immigration, but those have dipped to 27-point and 12-point advantages in the latest survey.

Democrats retained their edge on protecting constitutional rights (a 7-point advantage) and protecting democracy (11-point advantage), while their lead on dealing with health care dropped slightly from 23 percentage points in October to 20 percentage points in the new survey.
